items.mistake: Using a line chart to connect unordered categories on the x-axis
items.fix: Use a bar chart when categories have no natural order; reserve lines for time, epoch, or other meaningful sequences.
items.why: Lines imply continuity and trend between adjacent points—misleading for model names, countries, or arbitrary labels.
items.example: Plotting accuracy for ['LogReg', 'RF', 'XGB'] as a connected line suggests LogReg leads into RF temporally; use ax.bar instead.

items.mistake: Choosing scatter when the real question is distribution shape of one variable
items.fix: Histogram (or box plot across groups) for univariate or numeric-by-category questions; scatter only when two numerics are both analytically relevant.
items.why: Scatter against an index or ID adds no insight and obscures the univariate pattern you need for scaling decisions.
items.example: To inspect age skew, ax.hist(df['age']) — not ax.scatter(range(len(df)), df['age']).

items.mistake: Defaulting to pie charts for class proportions
items.fix: Use a bar chart of counts or proportions; human vision compares bar lengths more accurately than pie slices.
items.why: Pie charts hide small-class differences and fail with more than a few categories—common in imbalanced ML targets.
items.example: Five-class target balance: vertical bar of value_counts() beats a pie with thin slices for rare classes.

items.mistake: Plotting a correlation heatmap when the question is pairwise nonlinear relationship
items.fix: Heatmap for matrix overview; follow up with scatter (or joint plot in Seaborn) for specific high-|r| or suspicious pairs.
items.why: Correlation summarizes linear association only; a hot cell does not reveal curvature or heteroscedasticity.
items.example: After heatmap flags age vs income, scatter with alpha reveals a ceiling effect a single r value missed.
